summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the respondent withheld the child from spending time with the applicant father – Where the respondent holds an implacable belief that the applicant represents an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse to the child – Where the respondent is currently affected by a mental disorder impairing her ability to parent – Where the respondent is the child’s primary attachment figure but presents an unacceptable risk of harm to the child – Where the child has a positive relationship with the respondent – Where it was ordered that the child live with the applicant and spend no time with the respondent.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

This application concerns the parenting arrangements for X who is presently about 11 years of age. She is the only child of the applicant and the respondent.
The course of this application has been far from straightforward and its history reflects the difficult circumstances, not of her making, in which X has found herself. The procedural history of this application will bring some necessary context to the outcomes that remain in dispute and the issues to be determined to inform those outcomes.

History

X was an infant when her parents separated in September 2015 after a relationship spanning about eight years. Parenting proceedings were commenced in the Federal Circuit Court of Australia (as it then was known) in 2018. Final consent orders were agreed and made in that court in September 2018. The orders provided for equal shared parental responsibility between the parties for long-term decision making for X. She was to live with the respondent, with her time with the applicant increasing incrementally until 1 January 2019, and then further increasing until 1 September 2019. From that date, X was to spend unsupervised time with the applicant on a structured fortnightly arrangement, including overnight weekend and mid‑week time, as well as additional time during school holidays and on special occasions. The orders also contemplated that the parents would review the arrangements by exchanging proposals after the Easter school holidays in 2020.
Things did not go smoothly. The parties returned to the Federal Circuit Court of Australia (as it was then known) in late 2019 on the respondent’s application for orders about which school X would attend in 2020. Orders relating to X’s schooling were made, along with a costs order against the applicant.
The parties’ co-parenting relationship remained fractious and things came to a head again in mid to late October 2020. X spent a weekend with the applicant, but as a result of things that she is alleged to have said afterward and other observations made by the respondent of X’s behaviour, the respondent ceased any further time for X with the applicant. She was concerned that the applicant may have sexually abused X. She caused investigations to be commenced by relevant authorities.
The applicant then commenced these proceedings in late 2020 in the Federal Circuit Court of Australia (as it was then known). The earlier consent orders for X to spend time with the applicant were suspended. By and by, the proceedings were transferred to this court (then known as the Family Court of Australia), an independent children’s lawyer was appointed and the application was designated Magellan.
On 24 May 2021, the court undertook a hearing of what is described in the reasons that followed, as a discrete hearing as to risk, (see Allan & Sacco [2022] FedCFamC1F 120). Judgment on the discrete issue was reserved. The respondent was given leave to re-open her case and lead further evidence, and did so, on 17 August 2021. At the conclusion of argument, the applicant and the independent children’s lawyer contended that the applicant did not present an unacceptable risk of harm to X. The respondent contended to the contrary.
The court delivered its reasons and orders about the risk issue in March 2022 and found, for the reasons therein expressed, that the applicant did not present a risk of sexual harm to X “at all”.
The risk findings did nothing to quell the dispute between the parties and they were unable to settle their dispute. The respondent did not accept the court’s conclusions on the risk issue. X, who had spent no time with the applicant since the cessation of time in October 2020, continued to have no time with the applicant.
A further trial took place to determine what final parenting orders should be made for X. Reasons were delivered and final orders were made in January 2024 (see Allan & Sacco (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC1F 15). The orders provided for:
(a) the parties to have equal shared parental responsibility for major long‑term decisions affecting X;

(b) X to live with the respondent;

(c) X to spend time with the applicant, which was to progressively increase over time, beginning with professionally facilitated reconnection sessions involving a family report writer and therapeutic support and ending in extended overnight arrangements aligned with the Queensland school terms and holidays;

(d) other matters such as telephone communication, changeover arrangements and the use of a parenting communication application to minimise conflict.

The respondent appealed those orders. Their implementation was stayed until the outcome of the appeal. The appeal was allowed, the orders made in January 2024 were set aside and the application was remitted for rehearing by an order of the Full Court made on 20 August 2024. X continued to spend no time with applicant.
Notwithstanding her success on the appeal, the respondent applied for special leave to appeal to the High Court of Australia in respect of the Full Court’s order for remitter. She also sought a stay of the Full Court’s order for remitter pending her application for special leave. Unsurprisingly, the stay application was refused, as was special leave.
Attempts to list the application for rehearing swiftly met with resistance from the respondent who argued that her application for special leave to appeal and her subsequent appeal to the High Court of Australia needed to be heard and determined first.
Nonetheless, the remitted application was listed for trial before me and commenced on 13 January 2025. At that time, the respondent no longer had the benefit of legal representation.
At the outset, counsel for the independent children’s lawyer applied for an order that the respondent make X available to speak with the independent children’s lawyer. Despite requests, the respondent had failed to agree to the independent children’s lawyer’s request. In answer to the application, the respondent steadfastly refused to make X available on the basis that she had been significantly traumatised by the interviews in which she had been engaged thus far, and the respondent could countenance no more.
For reasons that I delivered on 13 January 2025, I dismissed the respondent’s opposition to the independent children’s lawyer’s application and ordered that the respondent produce X to the independent children’s lawyer at a specified time and place the following morning. However, she refused to comply with that order.
Evidence in the trial proceeded on 13 and 14 January 2025. Submissions were made on 15 January 2025. Until that point, the case for both the applicant and the independent children’s lawyer had been that X should continue to reside with the respondent and that X should spend increasing time with the applicant until a point where she would be spending equal time with each parent.
However, in submissions both the applicant and the independent children’s lawyer submitted that, having regard to the evidence given by two experts, and in the cross-examination of one in particular, the only outcome that would be in X’s best interests would be for her to live with the applicant, notwithstanding that she had not spent any time with him since October 2020. This represented a significant departure from their previously stated positions. The respondent’s position was that X should remain living with her and spend no time with the applicant. Although she said that she did not know if X had been sexually abused by the applicant, she thought that he nonetheless presented an unacceptable risk of such. She contended that X should spend no time with him until X had been counselled and the allegations “dealt with”.
In circumstances where there had been a wholesale change in the orders sought by the applicant, I considered that it was imperative that there be a report by a family consultant who had seen X and the applicant together and who was asked to comment on the new proposal. Accordingly, I made an order for the preparation of a report pursuant to s 62G(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) and adjourned the application for a few weeks.
On 18 February 2025, I made specific orders about the time and place of the interviews for the report and orders about X’s attendance. The expert chosen to prepare the report was Ms JJ, a highly experienced consultant social worker. I adjourned the final hearing of the application to 4 April 2025 for the purposes of receiving the report from Ms JJ and to take any further evidence or submissions the parties wished to lead or make.
In the meantime, the respondent suffered a deterioration in her mental health. She was admitted to mental health facilities on at least three, perhaps four, occasions leading up to the resumed hearing date.
The day before the resumed hearing, on the application of the independent children’s lawyer, I made an order that X be delivered to Court Children’s Services on the morning of the resumed hearing so that she could spend a short session with Ms JJ, who was to give evidence that day and so that after the proceeding concluded, Ms JJ could explain to X what, if anything was going to happen.
On 4 April 2025, the respondent did not appear. I was told that she had been admitted to hospital for certain mental health conditions on at least four occasions since the application had last been before me. Although the applicant did not appear, her sister Ms HH did attend with X. She and her husband had taken X to meet with the independent children’s lawyer a few days earlier and had brought her to court in accordance with the order I had made the day before the resumption of the trial.
Ms JJ had not had time to prepare and circulate a written report. She attended court on the morning of the resumed hearing and before the trial commenced she met again with X and the applicant.
After hearing submissions from counsel for the independent children’s lawyer, supported by counsel for the applicant, I resolved to take Ms JJ’s evidence orally, hear any submissions any party, including Ms HH, might wish to make, and then to make an interim order pending some clarity about the respondent’s capacity and mental health.
What follows is the barest summary of Ms JJ’s extensive and very helpful evidence.
Ms JJ said that at her initial interview, X clearly and consistently expressed a strong wish to resume and maintain time with the applicant, including overnight and holiday time. Ms JJ said that X identified sadness and distress about not seeing the applicant for over four years. She reported no fear or concern about spending time with the applicant and her expressed primary concern was missing the respondent and pets.
According to Ms JJ, X has no memory of the matters that have caused the respondent so much worry. Although X was interviewed by the police at the end of 2020, she has no memory of the interview. She told Ms JJ that no one has touched her inappropriately. X identified the applicant as a safe person she would tell if anything inappropriate occurred.
Ms JJ assessed that it was appropriate to reintroduce X to the applicant. X’s response to that news was to jump up from where she was sitting with her maternal grandfather and to express her excitement about that to the respondent. It is worth setting out Ms JJ’s observations of this first meeting as given in evidence by her (Transcript 4 April 2025, p.238 line 45 to p.239 line 12):
When I returned, Mr [Allan] was barely through the door before [X] leapt up from the sofa to embrace him. Mr [Allan] reciprocated and they hugged for some time. I actually took a photo of – some photos during these sessions, which I’ve put on a USB for the court. Mr [Allan] and [X] sat on the couch, and they talked about how good it was to see each other. They also acknowledged that they both felt a little bit awkward. They discussed how old [R’s] – [X’s] stepsisters were and that [Y] was going to be graduating from school this year. [X] continued to tell her father that it was nice to see him after so long, and she would spontaneously, throughout the whole hour, just reach across and hug him. She chatted away effortlessly and told her father that she had so much to catch up with him. She talked about the 10 teeth that she had lost over the years, her favourite colours, the movies that she liked, the NAPLAN tests that she was doing. When her father asked her about her holidays [X] responded with, “I would like to call your place home again. If I come back, I will also hang out with [Y] as I’m not so young anymore.
Because of the time that had elapsed since X had spent any time with the applicant, Ms JJ conducted several follow up sessions with X and the applicant. She observed warm, affectionate, child focused interactions between them. She saw X initiate physical affection, sustained conversation and express joy and relief at reunification. Interactions between X and the applicant’s now wife and their children were described as positive, affectionate, natural and evidence of the existence of meaningful sibling and stepfamily relationships.
Ms JJ assessed that X had a strong primary attachment to the respondent and she presented as affectionate and secure in the respondent’s presence. Ms JJ gave evidence that X is aware of the respondent’s hospitalisations and her perception that her mother remains “not herself.” Ms JJ assessed X as emotionally confused and was able to perceive “discomfort” from the respondent regarding contact with the applicant. Ms JJ identified a risk of emotional harm for X if she later realises that the absence of contact with the applicant resulted from her mother’s unilateral decision, rather than some court decision.
Ms JJ gave some evidence about what she had come to learn about the respondent’s mental health after speaking with some of the respondent’s health care providers and reading documents produced under subpoena. She said that the respondent had experienced multiple involuntary psychiatric admissions in early 2025, including a suicide attempt by self‑harm. She had been diagnosed with a brief psychotic episode due to psychosocial stressors, treated with olanzapine, later administered by injection under a treatment order. There was evidence of fluctuating mental health, impaired insight, thought blocking, paranoia and an ongoing risk of self‑harm. Ms JJ expressed concerns about the respondent’s capacity to safely parent if unsupervised.
Ms JJ assessed:
(a) the applicant as protective, child focused, and supportive of X's relationship with the respondent; and

(b) the respondent as having limited insight, difficulty facilitating the father-child relationship and a fixed belief system inconsistent with investigative outcomes.

Ms JJ recommended, on an interim basis, that:
(a) the respondent have an independent psychiatric assessment to ensure that X’s future wellbeing is not compromised;

(b) consideration be given to X living with the applicant for the next few months until a psychiatric assessment became available and any other pertinent information was available to the court to ensure that X’s future was not jeopardised;

(c) the applicant have sole parental responsibility for decision-making relating to X’s welfare during the time that she is in his full-time care;

(d) the applicant be required to take three weeks leave from his job to ensure that he is available to X 24 hours a day to support her transition into his care;

(e) consideration be given for X’s time and communication with the respondent be suspended to enable X’s placement with the applicant to be consolidated;

(f) if the court concludes that the respondent does not present a risk of harm for X, then the respondent’s time with X be supervised at a contact centre until final orders are made;

(g) X re-engages with Dr S to support her transition to the applicant’s care and the issues otherwise identified in Ms JJ evidence; and

(h) Ms JJ explain the orders and outcomes to X following the hearing.

I then took some very brief submissions from the independent children’s lawyer and counsel for the applicant who advocated for orders roughly aligning with those suggested by Ms JJ. I also sought input from Ms HH, who declined to add anything. I set out what I proposed and invited any submissions on my foreshadowed course. There were none. I invited counsel to prepare draft orders. They did so and presented them to Ms HH for her information and input also.
I expressed my satisfaction that the orders presented were in X’s best interests in the circumstances. I delivered brief oral reasons for that conclusion. I made interim orders providing:
(a) the applicant with sole decision-making responsibility for X;

(b) for X to live with the applicant;

(c) for X to spend no time with the respondent;

(d) an obligation on the applicant to facilitate communication at X's request with members of her maternal family (other than the respondent), by phone or electronic means, at reasonable times;

(e) for the maternal family (excluding the respondent) to communicate with X;

(f) for X to attend upon Dr S for therapeutic services as required, with arrangements to be organised by the applicant; and

(g) for X to attend a specified school.

I adjourned the proceedings to a future date so that further information about the respondent’s capacity could be obtained.
On 1 August 2025, the proceedings were adjourned to 17 October 2025 for completion of the trial of the proceedings. The parties were required to file a minute of the orders each was seeking prior to the resumption of the trial.

The ambit of the dispute

On 8 October 2025, the applicant filed a minute of the orders he sought on a final basis. They more or less aligned with the interim orders I made on 4 April 2025.
So too, the independent children’s lawyer filed a minute of the orders he submitted were in X’s best interests. They departed in only immaterial respects from the orders sought by the applicant.
The respondent did not file a minute of the orders she sought.
The trial resumed on 17 October 2025. Both parties and the independent children’s lawyer were represented by counsel.
The independent children’s lawyer tendered a memorandum he had prepared following a meeting with X a few days before the resumed hearing. It is important and I set it out in full:
As per my obligation, I spoke to [X] on 9th October 2025 and can outline the

following:
When asked to describe what was it like living with dad, [X] responded by saying “pretty good”.
When asked if she felt safe at dad’s house, [X] said that she felt safe.
When asked about school, [X] indicated that she was going “good”. [X] informed me that she recently got 2 awards. One for getting 7 x A’s and 1 x C and the other for her effort being exemplary (as opposed to commendable). She was also aware of a future award for speech later this month.
[X] informed me that she [...]turned 11 and enjoyed the sleepover party she had.
When asked to describe how her life was going, she answered by saying “going good”, then paused and then followed by “except missing mum”.
When asked if she would like the Court to know anything, she indicated “nothing off the top of my head”.
When specifically asked about her mother, and if she would like the Court to know anything in that regard, [X] responded as follows:
“I guess so – don’t know”
“Actually yeah”
“If my mum is not getting better, then a call every now and then” – “maybe 1/week – whatever Judge decides”
When asked to explain her phrase “not getting better”, [X] said “if she (mum) is getting better but not 100%”
When asked to explain the context of the phrase “getting better”, [X] responded by saying “healthier – her (mum’s) mental health”
Without prompting, [X] said “maybe it will never go away”
When asked what if it doesn’t go away, [X] said “I don’t know”.
[X] appeared to be processing information whilst talking and without prompting said “if my mum is not well enough to take care of me, then I could see her a certain amount of time and then go back to dad”.
In exploring how to see her mother, I asked [X] if she knew what a contact centre was? [X] didn’t so I explained it to her. Having explained to [X] how a contact centre works, [X’s] response was as follows:
“I wouldn’t mind that”
“But I wouldn’t be comfortable if someone was listening to our conversation”
“But if that’s the only way, then that’s the only way”.
When asked, if not a contact centre then how, [X] responded by saying “I don’t know”.
The independent children’s lawyer led written evidence in the form of a sworn report from Dr KK, forensic psychiatrist, about the state of the respondent’s mental health. Dr KK summarised her opinion by saying:
I interviewed Ms [Sacco] a 40-year-old, single, Caucasian female, and mother to one child, for the purpose of preparing this psychiatric medicolegal assessment report.
She presented with mental health symptoms that would meet the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th Edition) DSM-5 diagnostic criteria for:
• Delusional disorder

• Alcohol use disorder in early remission
Causing functional impairment and distress.
This was further supported by psychometric testing.
Of note were inconsistencies in Ms [Sacco’s] history, collateral information, presentation, reported subjective experiences, objective assessment, and psychometric testing, as well as the MCMI-IV low disclosure scores and high desirability scores.
I am of the opinion that Ms [Sacco] has impaired capacity to be a sole parent, and to co-parent effectively in the best interest of her child.
Dr KK was cross-examined by counsel for the parties. She did not depart from her opinions although accepted a proposition put by counsel for the respondent that if some evidence existed to justify an inflexibly held belief, it could not be said that the belief was delusional.
Ms JJ who gave evidence on 4 April 2025, also gave further evidence on 17 October 2025. She confirmed that she had seen and read Dr KK’s report and a memorandum prepared by the independent children’s lawyer following a visit that he had with X on 7 October 2025. Having seen that material, when asked whether there was any change she wished to make to her previous opinions, she said (Transcript 17 October 2025, p.8 lines 26-42):
Yes, there is. I was extremely concerned after reading Dr [JJ’s] report, and particularly in terms of the diagnosis of the delusional beliefs and her – her disorder. I was also extremely concerned when I read paragraph 159 where Dr [JJ] says, “I’m of the opinion that a specific risk of kidnapping and fleeing with the child, as well as the risk of filicide and suicide would be significantly elevated”, and she goes on to talk about – that the risk could be that the mother reacts in an erratic manner, which she has before when she slashed her wrist, and that this could include the stresses of learning about the outcome of Dr [JJ’s] report and any outcomes from Court. So I guess that that has led me to feel that I would change my recommendations and my recommendation for the mother to see the child in a supervised capacity in the future. The concern I have about that is that I don’t believe that a supervision centre would have the capacity to stop the mother if she decided to abscond with the child. I think that’s an unreasonable request to put on the contact centre and they would be very reluctant to do that. So I – I guess at this point in time, until the mother’s mental health is significantly improved, I would be recommending that the child remain living with the father and have no contact with the mother.
Ms JJ went on to say that given the significance of her relationship with the respondent to X, some correspondence two or three times a year via X’s therapist would be appropriate.
Ms JJ was cross-examined by counsel for the parties and briefly in re-examination by counsel for the independent children’s lawyer. The independent children’s lawyer’s counsel then made some brief submissions. So too did counsel for each of the parties. The submissions did not canvass the question of what, if any, risk of sexual abuse the applicant presented to X. That issue had effectively been dealt with on 4 April 2025 for the reasons I gave on that day. However, I say some more about this issue later in these reasons.
Counsel for the independent children’s lawyer submitted that the applicant should have sole decision‑making responsibility for X, X live with the applicant and that X spend no time with the respondent. She suggested that there be limited communication between X and the respondent and then only by way of indirect correspondence, to be managed through a therapeutic intermediary, so that the applicant is not the gatekeeper of communication. The independent children’s lawyer supported orders that would facilitate communication between X and the maternal family (excluding the respondent) such communication to occur at X’s request.
To support these orders, counsel for the independent children’s lawyer pointed to the expert evidence that demonstrated that the respondent’s capacity to co‑parent is significantly impaired due to her delusional disorder and in particular her beliefs about the risk posed by the applicant.
The independent children’s lawyer submitted that the evidence demonstrated that X is settled and thriving in the applicant’s care, as reflected by her recorded views, her school progress and engagement in therapeutic support. The evidence, indeed, established those matters. At no point was the respondent able to identify any concerns with the applicant’s parenting beyond her belief in sexual risk.
Counsel for the independent children’s lawyer submitted that the order for no time between X and the respondent was appropriate on the evidence and in particular was justified by Ms JJ’s view that supervised or contact‑centre arrangements were impractical and insufficient to manage the risks that had been identified by Dr KK, given the nature of the respondent’s mental health condition and the difficulty in assessing fluctuations in her functioning. Counsel acknowledged that according to Ms JJ the respondent has managed not to expose X to her beliefs, but nonetheless the risk of harm if this were to change is significant and moreover, that went nowhere towards negating the other elevated risks identified by Dr KK.
Counsel for the respondent confirmed that the respondent’s position was that X should live with her and spend no time with the applicant because he represented an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse to her. However, she acknowledged that I had previously determined that the applicant did not represent an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse to X and that she had been living with the applicant since April 2025. She submitted that she could point to nothing that would warrant a reconsideration of that issue. Nonetheless the respondent’s position remained.
Counsel for the respondent suggested that some of the orders already in place could be strengthened so as to protect X from any unnecessary instability in her schooling. She emphasised the respondent’s role in X’s life and that on the evidence X was worried about the respondent. She argued that an order for some communication between X and the respondent was appropriate so that X would know her mother was doing okay. In that respect, she emphasised that the evidence demonstrated that the respondent had been able to shield X from her own views about the applicant.
Counsel for the applicant affirmed his client’s position. His submissions followed the same theme as those of the independent children’s lawyer’s. He submitted that the applicant was content for an order that followed the draft minute of orders proposed by the independent children’s lawyer.

Consideration

When the trial started, the focus of the evidence was upon the proposition that, by reason of what X had said so many years ago and on the basis of observations of her behaviour by the respondent and others, the applicant represented an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse to her. The proposition that the applicant was an unacceptable risk of sexual harm to X was an inference drawn by the respondent from X’s words and behaviours.
However, the evidence did not bear out that he presented such a risk. Whilst on their face X’s statements to the respondent and to the police in her 93A interview might indicate some form of abuse, they do not necessarily indicate that. Her statements and her behaviours were equally consistent with innocent explanations such as the applicant’s explanation of washing X once in the shower. The respondent’s concerns were the subject of investigation by the police and others, and they were found to be hollow. There was no basis to take any action at all against the applicant.
X has been consulting with Dr S, a psychologist in private practice since 2021. Dr S gave evidence by way of a report and by way of oral evidence. Dr S’s evidence emphasised the distress and confusion that X was observed to be suffering as a result of not be able to pursue her relationship with the applicant. She described the strategies that X had employed to deal with her grief over the loss of that relationship, including devising the fantasy that her father had died. X wished to see and spend time with the applicant. X never expressed any fear or concern about the applicant and made no complaint to Dr S about behaviour that might in any way be seen as abusive. Dr S said that X had no memory of talking to the police about the matters that concerned the respondent.
Nonetheless, the respondent continued to express during the course of the trial the belief that X may have been sexually abused by the applicant. She would not countenance any other possibility, although purported to say that she did not know whether what X had said was true or not. Indeed, on more than one occasion the respondent suggested that once X had seen a therapist about her allegations and they had been “dealt with”, time with the applicant could resume. She did not explain what she meant by the proposition that the allegations needed to be “dealt with”, but it is clear that the work that Dr S had done with X was not enough.
Subsequent evidence, such as the respondent’s statements to Ms JJ and to Dr KK, demonstrate that her belief is implacable. According to that belief, X is at an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse in the applicant’s care.
For the reasons that I gave on 4 April 2025, I reject the proposition that X is at an unacceptable risk of harm from the applicant. The observational evidence upon which the respondent relies is consistent with innocent explanation. There is no support for the respondent’s belief in the evidence of any of the experts in the case, nor in the investigations carried out when the respondent reported her concerns to the authorities.
Whilst the evidence is that the respondent has not exposed X to her view that the applicant may have sexually abused her or that he presents a risk of such, what the respondent has done is to act on that belief such that from October 2020 she erected an impenetrable barrier between X and the applicant.
The severance of X’s relationship with the applicant in October 2020 caused her emotional harm. Dr S, X’s therapist, gave evidence in January 2025 that when X last saw the applicant she was outside of the early childhood attachment phase, which meant that she had a solidified attachment with the applicant. Dr S went on to catalogue the probable difficulties for X from the sudden severance of that relationship. Dr S’s evidence on that matter was not challenged. X suffered immediate distress which was still apparent when she first started consulting Dr S in January 2021 and which continued until her final consultations in 2024.
I am satisfied that the respondent’s failure to facilitate X’s relationship with the applicant was unjustified in the sense that he did not represent an unacceptable risk of harm to her. More than that, the respondent’s actions caused X emotional and psychological harm in the ways described by Dr S. R’s statements to Ms JJ in early 2025 demonstrate that whilst the respondent was perhaps able to insulate X from her beliefs about the applicant, that did not insulate her from the harm that was likely to be caused and was caused by severing her relationship with the applicant. It simply caused more confusion on X’s part. She did not know why it was that she was unable to spend time with her father. She was clearly very anxious to do so.
I am satisfied that were it the case that X lived in the full-time care of the respondent she would not have the opportunity for any relationship with the applicant. Without shifting her view about the risk the applicant poses to X, the respondent will not foster, encourage or facilitate a relationship between them and X will be exposed to the very real risk and range of long-term harms described by Dr S.
When Dr S was cross-examined, the applicant’s case was that X should remain with the respondent and spend increasingly longer amounts of time with him until she was living in an equal time arrangement between the parties. Dr S was asked, however, about the available options in the event that the court decided there was no unacceptable risk of harm from the applicant but the court concluded that the respondent would not support X’s relationship with the applicant. Dr S identified three options, each of which required the respondent to undertake some form of therapy or treatment so as to shift her implacable view that the applicant was a risk. The third option suggested that X live with the applicant and she spend no time with the respondent until such time as the respondent had undertaken that treatment or therapy.
Dr S pointed out that the third option would, if the respondent was unable to shift her view about the applicant, probably see X lose her relationship with the respondent. That too, would cause her distress and harm. But those harms could be mitigated, in part at least, by providing opportunities for X to maintain some contact with the respondent. Unlike the respondent, I did not form the impression that the applicant would not ensure X had time and space for her relationship with the respondent if the court made orders to facilitate that. I am satisfied that if orders are in place for the purposes of ensuring that X maintains a relationship with the respondent at some level, the applicant will see that the orders are honoured. This third option would also provide protection from the risks identified by Dr KK arising from the respondent’s delusional disorder and her consequently impaired parenting capacity.
Orders for X to live with the applicant will do more to promote X’s safety than orders for her to live with the respondent. She will be protected from the emotional and psychological harm about which both Dr S and Ms JJ gave evidence. Orders for her to live with the respondent will not promote her safety as well. She will not have her relationship with the applicant fostered in the way in which it ought to be. Moreover, if X is to live with the applicant it permits of the possibility that X might indeed have a relationship with the respondent, although in a way that protects her from the risks identified by Dr S (the loss of a relationship with her mother) and Dr KK (the risks identified in her report).
Orders that will permit X to have a relationship with the applicant coincide with X’s long held and oft expressed views. An order that she be permitted to live with the applicant is consistent with her lived experience since 4 April 2025. Her statements to the independent children’s lawyer most recently (see Exhibit 15) are also consistent with such orders.
Orders which would see X living with the respondent will not meet her psychological and emotional needs. She will be exposed to the risks of harm about which Dr S gave evidence. X will not be permitted to have a relationship with the applicant. She will also be exposed to the risks about which Dr KK gave evidence. Whilst she might be exposed to some psychological or emotional harm living in the applicant’s household because she might not be able to pursue her relationship with the respondent safely (and thereby not be able to have all of her emotional or psychological needs met), the likelihood of that harm arising is small given the evidence about how well X has settled into the applicant’s care and the likelihood that he will promote her relationship with the respondent if and when it is safe to do so.
I am satisfied that the applicant has an uncompromised capacity to meet X’s developmental, psychological and emotional needs. Evidence of that is the fact that when this trial began, he simply wanted orders that would see X spending some time with him and she otherwise living with the respondent. He recognised the importance and primacy of X’s relationship with the respondent. The respondent, by contrast has an impaired capacity to meet X’s developmental, psychological and emotional needs for the reasons given by Dr KK.
It is clear from the evidence of Dr S, Ms JJ and the applicant that X’s relationships with her extended maternal and paternal families, including her stepmother and step-siblings (and her half-sibling R) are important to her. I am satisfied that the applicant appreciates the significance of those relationships to X and will work to ensure that they are respected and permitted to be maintained and develop.

Conclusions

The evidence of both the applicant and the respondent was that their communication was non-existent. Moreover, the respondent harbours delusional beliefs about the applicant and the risk he poses to X. In those circumstances I consider that any order other than that the applicant have sole parental responsibility for making long-term decisions for X is untenable. The applicant should carry the burden of such an order alone.
As I have already stated, the orders promoted by the independent children’s lawyer and the applicant that X live with the applicant best suit X’s needs in this case. There will be an order that she live with the applicant.
I do not accept that X should not have any contact with the respondent. Her relationship with the respondent is important to X and provided it can happen in a safe way, it should happen. I have considered making an order that X spend supervised time at a contact centre (or in some other professionally supervised setting) with the respondent. But I accept Ms JJ’s reasons for rejecting such an idea. I am not satisfied given the content of Dr KK’s opinion that professionally supervised time between X and the respondent will, at this point, likely mitigate the identified risks for X. I have however, made orders more or less in accord with Ms JJ’s suggestion that the respondent be permitted to correspond with X a few times per year. I have also made an order that X may correspond with the respondent.
Moreover, both Dr S and Dr KK make suggestions about what it is that the respondent might do to deal with her delusional beliefs, although Dr KK gave evidence that delusional disorders are difficult to treat. Nonetheless, the prospect remains and it is open to the respondent to present appropriate evidence of having dealt with those beliefs. In that event, she might either reach an agreement with the applicant for X to spend some time with her, or, if necessary, approach the court for appropriate relief.
The balance of the orders sought by the independent children’s lawyer are unremarkable.
I make the orders set out at the commencement of these reasons.
